content.headline: A Letter From the Man Who Refused to Call His Kids for Help
content.subheadline: How a retired contractor built a 2-pound blower that outperforms $300 gas models — and why he's offering it at cost this week only

sections.body_html:
Dear friend,

I need to tell you something I've never shared publicly before. Four years ago, on a Saturday morning in October, I walked into my garage, wrapped both hands around my old Husqvarna gas blower — the same one I'd used for fifteen years — and put it back down after thirty seconds. My lower back seized up so hard I had to sit on a bucket for ten minutes before I could walk inside.

I was 64 years old. I'd built houses with my own two hands for over three decades. And I couldn't blow leaves off my own driveway.

I sat in that garage for a long time. Not because of the pain — I'd dealt with worse on job sites. I sat there because of what it meant. And if you're reading this letter, I suspect you already know exactly what I mean.

visual_placeholder.description: A silver-haired man in his mid-60s sitting on a step inside a garage, looking down at a heavy gas blower resting on the floor beside him. Morning light comes through the open garage door. The mood is quiet and reflective — not defeated, just thinking.

sections.heading: My name is Tom Haskell. And I almost gave up on doing things myself.
sections.body_html:
I grew up watching my father maintain every inch of our property with his own hands. Not because he had to — because it was who he was. That pride passed down to me, and for forty years, I never thought twice about it. I mowed, I raked, I blew, I shoveled. My garage was organized like a hardware store. I was the guy the neighbors called when they needed advice on anything.

Then somewhere around my early sixties, things started to shift in ways I didn't expect. The gas blower that never bothered me before suddenly felt like lifting a cement block. After twenty minutes, my shoulders would ache. After thirty, I'd need a full day to recover. My wife started suggesting we hire someone to handle the yard. I smiled and said maybe. And I quietly went to bed that night feeling like something important was slipping away from me — something I hadn't given a name to yet, but could feel clearly.

I want to be honest with you, because I think you deserve honesty: I started avoiding certain jobs. Not because I didn't know how to do them — because the thought of the pain the next morning stopped me before I even started. Leaves would pile up on the back patio for a week before I worked up the motivation to deal with them. My driveway looked like I'd given up on it. And every time I walked past it, I felt something I hadn't expected to feel at my age: a quiet, stubborn shame.

That October morning in the garage was my breaking point. I wasn't going to accept this. I wasn't ready to hand my house — my home, the thing I'd spent thirty years maintaining with my own hands — over to someone else. So I did what I'd always done when something didn't work right: I started building a better way.

visual_placeholder.description: Two-part image: on the left, a heavy gas leaf blower sitting alone on a concrete garage floor, dusty and imposing. On the right, a silver-haired man standing tall on a clean driveway, holding the Seese Pro in one relaxed hand at his side, smiling with confidence. Autumn trees in the background.

sections.heading: What I built — and why it surprised even me
sections.body_html:
I spent the better part of two years talking to engineers, ordering parts, and destroying prototypes in my own backyard. I wanted one thing that no blower on the market seemed willing to give me: enough power to actually move wet leaves and compacted debris, in a tool light enough to hold with one hand without my back filing a complaint the next morning. Every blower I tested made me choose between the two. Heavy and powerful, or light and useless. That compromise had always bothered me, and now I refused to live with it.

What we eventually built — what I now call the Seese Pro — weighs 950 grams. That's just over two pounds. Less than a bag of sugar. You hold it the same way you'd hold a cordless drill, one hand, arm relaxed at your side. But here's the part that still surprises me: it pushes air at over 200 miles per hour. That's more airspeed than most gas blowers I used on construction sites in my thirties. The brushless motor runs at 30,000 RPM — the same motor technology used in performance drones and electric vehicles. No gas mixing. No extension cord to trip over. No choke, no pull-cord, no ritual. You pick it up, press the trigger, and it works.

The first time I cleared my back patio — 400 square meters of flagstone I used to spend a morning on — I timed myself. Four minutes and forty seconds. My back felt nothing. I wasn't even breathing hard. I stood there for a moment, just looking at the clean stone, and I laughed out loud. My wife came outside to see what was funny. I told her I'd just gotten my Saturday mornings back. Then I walked over to the car, still wet from a wash I'd done the day before, and dried it completely in just over three minutes — every door gap, every mirror edge, every wheel arch — without a single towel touching the paint.

visual_placeholder.description: Tom, or a similar active senior man, standing on a clean flagstone patio holding the Seese Pro loosely in one hand, arm relaxed. Autumn leaves visible at the far edge of the patio, recently blown away. Warm morning light. The posture is calm and proud — a man who just finished a job he's satisfied with.

sections.heading: Why I'm writing to you today
sections.body_html:
I'll be straight with you, because that's the only way I know how to do business. I'm not a corporation. I'm a retired contractor who built something in his garage because he needed it, and then started making it for other people who needed it too. We've kept our prices as honest as we can manage, but a few times a year, we do what I call a cost run — we move through as much inventory as we can at the steepest discount we're able to offer, to put it in front of the people who genuinely need it most. This week is one of those weeks. And I wanted to write to you directly, rather than just run an ad, because I think you deserve to know the story behind it.

I got an email last month that I've read more times than I'd care to admit out loud.

"Tom, I almost didn't buy it. I thought I was being foolish, spending money on another gadget at my age. I'm 71 and I've been doing yard work for forty years — I didn't think I needed help. But my son kept looking at me like I was about to hurt myself every time I picked up the old blower. The Seese Pro changed everything. I do my whole yard myself now, no pain, no drama, no asking anyone for anything. My son stopped worrying. And honestly? I feel like myself again."
— Email from Margaret, 71, Columbus OH

That email is why I keep doing this. Margaret wasn't looking for a gadget. She was fighting to stay herself — capable, independent, the person who takes care of her own home without asking anyone for help. That fight is real, and I understand it in my bones. It's the same fight I was having in that garage four years ago. The Seese Pro didn't just solve a yard maintenance problem for me. It gave me back something I didn't have a word for at the time, but I think you know exactly what I mean.

visual_placeholder.description: Close-up of older, weathered but strong hands — a craftsman's hands — holding the Seese Pro gently. Soft-focus autumn garden in the background. The image feels personal and intimate, not commercial.

sections.heading: Here's what you get when you order this week
sections.body_html:
When you order the Seese Pro today, you get the complete kit: the blower with the 21V 3.0Ah lithium battery and fast charger already included, plus three free bonus accessories — including a compact carrying case valued at $25 — all brought to you at $119.99 instead of the regular $249.99. That's 55% off, no coupon needed, applied automatically at checkout. There's no subscription, no upsell trap, no hidden anything. You buy it once and it's yours — backed by our lifetime replacement warranty. If it ever breaks down for any reason, we replace it. Full stop.

I know $119.99 isn't pocket change. But consider what you've already spent on a gas blower that punishes your back, on car-drying towels and chamois, on a windshield scraper you dig out every winter, on a shop vacuum for the garage dust. The Seese Pro replaces every single one of those. And it runs on a charge that lasts 40 minutes — enough for a full yard cleanup or a complete car detail in a single session. If after thirty days it's sitting unused in your garage, I want you to send it back. I'll refund every dollar, no questions asked. I've honored that guarantee with every customer since the first day we shipped, and I intend to keep honoring it.

visual_placeholder.description: Clean flat-lay product shot: Seese Pro blower, 21V battery, fast charger, and compact carrying case arranged on a light neutral surface. The case is open to show everything fits inside neatly. Badge overlay in the corner: '55% OFF — $119.99 today' and 'Lifetime Warranty Included'.

sections.body_html:
If any part of this letter sounds familiar — if you've ever put a tool back down because your body said no, or quietly avoided a job because you didn't want to deal with what came after — I hope you'll give the Seese Pro thirty days. You have nothing to lose except the leaves on your patio.
